meta.description: Resume feedback NLG sentence bank. Each signal has scored tiers (0-100). Each tier contains observation/interpretation/action sentence arrays — pick one per layer randomly at runtime for LLM-like variety. Use {{token}} for dynamic interpolation.

scoring.scale: 0–100 per signal. Signals are weighted and combined into a composite resume score.

filename.note: Hygiene signal. Low weight — doesn't affect hiring, but affects first impression.

bullet_quality.note: Impact and specificity of work bullets.

resume_length.note: Conciseness and respect for recruiter time.

anomalies.note: Credibility red flags (date errors, impossible claims).

overall_tailoring.note: How tuned this resume is to this specific JD.

observation: Your filename is clean, professional, and immediately identifies you.
The file is named correctly — your name and role are both present.
Your filename follows best practice: no versioning, no clutter, clear attribution.
interpretation: It signals care and professionalism before the document is even opened.
In a recruiter's downloads folder of 80 generic files, yours is identifiable at a glance.
Zero friction before they open it — a small but clean signal.

observation: Your filename includes your name but is missing the target role.
The filename is identifiable but could be more descriptive.
Your name is present in the filename, though the role context is absent.
interpretation: Better than most, but a recruiter won't know what role you're applying for from the filename alone.
Identifiable but not fully optimised — a minor missed opportunity.
Functional but not tailored. Close to best practice.
action: Update your filename to FirstName_LastName_RoleTitle.pdf — e.g. Jane_Smith_BackendEngineer.pdf
Add the target role to your filename. It takes 10 seconds and removes ambiguity.
Include the specific role you're applying for in the filename — makes it easier to track across multiple applications.

observation: Your filename is partially informative but missing key components.
The file name is functional but doesn't clearly identify you or the role.
Your filename hints at who this is but lacks enough detail to stand alone.
interpretation: A recruiter can guess, but shouldn't have to — the filename should do the work.
Passable, but it's a gap that's trivial to fix.
Not a dealbreaker, but it's an easy win left on the table.
action: Rename to FirstName_LastName_RoleTitle.pdf — all three components should be present.
A strong filename is: YourName_TargetRole.pdf. Add whichever component is missing.
Fix your filename: include your full name and the role you're applying for, separated by underscores.

observation: Your filename contains versioning markers like 'final', 'v2', or 'updated'.
There's no personal identifier in the filename — it can't be attributed to you without opening it.
Your filename looks like an internal draft, not a professional submission.
interpretation: Versioning words signal a disorganised submission process before the resume is read.
A recruiter saving this file has no idea whose resume it is — unnecessary friction.
It implies this wasn't prepared specifically for this application. First impressions matter.
action: Rename your file to FirstName_LastName_RoleTitle.pdf — strip all draft markers.
Remove version suffixes entirely. The submitted file should look deliberate, not iterated.
Use a clean, permanent name: YourName_Role.pdf. Leave the versioning for your local backup folder.

observation: Your resume is submitted as a {{file_extension}} file, not a PDF.
The file format is {{file_extension}} — not the industry standard for resume submissions.
A non-PDF format was detected. This creates ATS and formatting risk immediately.
interpretation: Most ATS systems parse non-PDF files inconsistently — your carefully formatted layout may be destroyed on ingestion.
.docx and similar formats render differently across systems. What you see may not be what the recruiter sees.
PDF is the universal standard because it locks formatting. Any other format introduces unnecessary risk.
action: Export as PDF before submitting. File > Export > PDF in Word, Google Docs, or Pages — takes one click.
Convert to PDF immediately. Never submit a .docx — formatting breaks across different systems and ATS tools.
Submit only as PDF. It's the only format that guarantees your layout, fonts, and structure are preserved everywhere.

observation: Your title '{{candidate_title}}' is an exact match for this role.
The headline on your resume — '{{candidate_title}}' — mirrors the job title precisely.
There's a clean, exact alignment between your stated title and '{{jd_title}}'.
interpretation: This is the first filter a recruiter applies — you pass it without hesitation.
No ambiguity, no inference required. The recruiter knows within two seconds you're relevant.
Exact title alignment removes the most common early rejection trigger.

observation: Your title '{{candidate_title}}' is adjacent to the target role '{{jd_title}}'.
The title on your resume is close to '{{jd_title}}' but not an exact match.
'{{candidate_title}}' reads as a near-neighbour to '{{jd_title}}' — one step away.
interpretation: Close enough that most recruiters will keep reading, but the slight mismatch was noted.
Adjacent titles pass the filter most of the time — but your skills section has to confirm the relevance quickly.
A small gap. Experienced recruiters understand title variations; junior screeners may not.
action: Bridge the gap in your summary: 'I've operated as a {{candidate_title}}, performing the core responsibilities of a {{jd_title}}.'
If you do this work under a different title, say so explicitly in your opening line.
Add a one-line clarifier in your summary that maps your title to the JD's language.

observation: Your title '{{candidate_title}}' is from a related domain but doesn't directly read as '{{jd_title}}'.
The recruiter sees '{{candidate_title}}' where they expected something closer to '{{jd_title}}'.
Your headline is in a related area but creates ambiguity about fit for this specific role.
interpretation: A recruiter will pause here — it's unclear from the title alone whether the required depth is present.
Related-domain titles require the recruiter to do extra work to confirm fit. Many won't bother.
You may be entirely qualified — but the title framing makes that hard to see quickly.
action: Add a subtitle or clarifying line under your name: e.g. '{{candidate_title}} — specialising in {{jd_title}} responsibilities'.
Use your summary to explicitly reframe your background in the language of this JD. Don't make the recruiter connect the dots.
Consider whether '{{jd_title}}' is an accurate reflection of your work. If it is, lead with it.

observation: Your title '{{candidate_title}}' doesn't immediately read as '{{jd_title}}' to a recruiter.
The headline on your resume and the job title are clearly misaligned.
There is a significant gap between how you've titled yourself and what this role requires.
interpretation: In a 10-second scan, a title mismatch often ends the review entirely.
Recruiters are pattern-matching fast — a mismatched title is a skip signal, not a pause signal.
Your skills may be perfect, but the title is the first gate — and this one isn't passing easily.
action: If you legitimately do this work, retitle your resume headline to '{{jd_title}}' or as close as is accurate.
Use your summary to close the gap fast: 'Although titled {{candidate_title}}, I have spent the last X years doing {{jd_title}} work across...'
Your skills section needs to compensate hard here — every required keyword must appear in the top third of the resume.

observation: No clear professional title was found in your resume header.
Your resume doesn't lead with a title — a recruiter has to read the whole document to understand what you do.
The title present — '{{candidate_title}}' — is unrelated to this role in any recognisable way.
interpretation: Without a clear title, the recruiter has to invest time they don't have to understand your fit.
A missing or irrelevant title is the highest rejection-risk signal at the top of a resume.
Most screeners will not continue past an unrelated or absent title — the first 5 seconds are lost.
action: Add a professional title directly below your name. This is non-negotiable.
Your header should read: Name → Title → Contact. Right now the title element is missing or wrong.
Choose the most accurate title that maps to this role and place it at the very top of your resume, immediately below your name.

observation: Your summary states your title, years of experience, core technical skills, and domain context.
The summary at the top of your resume is specific, technical, and immediately informative.
Your opening profile covers all the key signals: who you are, how long you've been doing it, and what you specialise in.
interpretation: This is exactly what a summary should do — it orients the recruiter in under two seconds.
A recruiter reading this summary can make a provisional fit decision before reaching your experience section.
Strong summaries like this reduce recruiter cognitive load. It does the positioning work so the rest of the resume confirms it.

observation: Your summary is present and substantive, though it's missing one or two key elements.
A solid summary is there — it covers your background but skips some specific signals.
Your profile section communicates your role clearly but doesn't fully surface your skills or domain.
interpretation: Better than most — a recruiter will read it. Small additions would make it significantly stronger.
It does the job at a surface level but leaves some positioning work to the reader.
A partial summary is far better than none, but the missing specifics are leaving value on the table.
action: Add what's missing — typically: years of experience, 2–3 core technical skills, and the domain you've worked in.
Tighten your summary to the formula: [Title] with [X] years in [domain], specialising in [2–3 skills].
Punch up the summary with one specific skill or domain anchor — something a recruiter can use to slot you immediately.

observation: Your summary uses soft skill language with no technical specifics.
The opening profile is present but reads as generic — phrases like 'results-driven' and 'team player' dominate.
Your summary says a lot without communicating anything concrete about your technical profile.
interpretation: Generic phrases like 'passionate problem solver' don't tell a technical recruiter anything about fit.
A summary full of soft skills in a technical role review is almost worse than no summary — it wastes the recruiter's attention.
Recruiters skim summaries for hard signals. Soft language registers as noise.
action: Rewrite your summary from scratch. Formula: [Title] with [X] years of experience in [domain], building [specific outputs] using [core tech stack].
Remove all generic adjectives. Replace every soft-skill phrase with a concrete technical one.
Try this template: 'Backend Engineer with 6 years building distributed Java/Spring Boot APIs, primarily in fintech and SaaS environments.' Then adjust for your reality.

observation: Your summary is present but too brief to communicate anything useful.
What's there reads as an objective statement rather than a professional profile.
The summary section exists but is essentially a single vague sentence.
interpretation: A one-line summary is a missed opportunity — recruiters spend the most time on the top third of a resume.
Objective statements ('Looking for a challenging role...') are outdated and tell the recruiter about your needs, not your value.
The summary slot is prime real estate. A thin entry there is a gap, not a feature.
action: Expand your summary to 2–3 lines. Cover: what you are, how long you've done it, what you've built, where you've done it.
Replace the objective statement with a profile statement. Objective = what you want. Profile = what you offer.
Two sentences minimum: one for title + YOE + domain, one for your core technical specialisation.

observation: No summary or profile section was found on your resume.
Your resume goes straight from contact details to work experience — there's no summary.
The top of your resume is missing a professional summary entirely.
interpretation: The recruiter had no narrative hook — they go straight to experience with no context about who you are.
Without a summary, the recruiter has to read your entire experience section to understand your positioning. Most won't.
A missing summary is the single easiest improvement to make. It's an unforced error.
action: Add a 2–3 line summary directly below your contact information. This is the highest-ROI addition you can make to this resume.

Write a profile using this formula: [Title] with [X] years of experience in [core skills]. Known for [specific strength or domain]. E.g. 'Backend Engineer with 5 years building Java/Spring Boot APIs at scale across fintech and SaaS.'
Don't skip the summary. It's the recruiter's orientation point — without it, your resume is context-free.

observation: Your {{calculated_yoe}} years of experience falls squarely within the {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} year requirement for this role.
At {{calculated_yoe}} years, you meet the experience requirement of {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} years with room to spare.
Experience level: {{calculated_yoe}} years — right in the target range of {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} years.
interpretation: You pass the experience filter without any caveats or asterisks.
No gaps, no overqualification flags, no calculation needed. Straight pass.
The dates are clean and the total lands exactly where recruiters want to see it for this role.

observation: Your {{calculated_yoe}} years of experience meets the {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} year requirement — calculated from your listed dates.
Based on your work history dates, you have approximately {{calculated_yoe}} years of experience, which aligns with this role.
The dates on your resume add up to {{calculated_yoe}} years — within the required range.
interpretation: The dates were clean enough to calculate quickly. No ambiguity in the timeline.
A recruiter had to do the maths here, but the result is positive — you qualify.
Calculating from dates adds minor friction, but the outcome is a pass.
action: State your total years of experience explicitly in your summary — remove the need for a recruiter to calculate it.
Add a line to your profile: '{{calculated_yoe}} years of experience in [domain].' Don't make the recruiter do arithmetic.
Consider stating your YOE directly in your headline or summary to make this a zero-effort read.

observation: At {{calculated_yoe}} years, you're slightly below the {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} year requirement.
Your experience — {{calculated_yoe}} years — is close to but just under the stated {{jd_yoe_min}} year minimum.
You fall slightly short of the {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} year requirement with {{calculated_yoe}} years of experience.
interpretation: Recruiters treat YOE requirements as guidelines, not hard rules — close is often close enough.
A minor gap here can be compensated by strong skills visibility and high-quality bullets.
You're within striking distance. The YOE flag is minor if the rest of your resume is strong.
action: Lead with quality over duration in your summary. Frame your experience around impact, not years.
If you have strong project work, open-source contributions, or freelance experience, make sure it's counted and dated.
Don't apologise for the gap — outperform on skills and impact instead.

observation: Your {{calculated_yoe}} years of experience is significantly below the {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} year requirement.
At {{calculated_yoe}} years, there's a meaningful gap against the {{jd_yoe_min}} year minimum this role asks for.
Your experience is described in vague terms — phrases like 'several years' rather than specific dates.
At {{calculated_yoe}} years, you may be overqualified for a role requiring {{jd_yoe_min}}–{{jd_yoe_max}} years.
interpretation: A significant YOE gap is one of the most common hard rejection filters — many recruiters won't proceed past it.
Vague experience language means a recruiter can't verify your seniority. They assume the worst and move on.
Overqualification flags are real — recruiters worry about salary expectations and long-term interest in a more junior scope.
action: Add explicit dates to every role (Month Year – Month Year). Remove all vague time references.
Consider targeting roles where your {{calculated_yoe}} years makes you a strong candidate, not a stretch.
If you are overqualified, address it directly in your cover letter — explain why this role is the right move for you.

observation: No dates were found on one or more roles — years of experience cannot be verified.
The chronology of your work history is inconsistent and cannot be reliably used to calculate experience.
Employment dates are absent from your resume — a recruiter cannot determine your experience level.
interpretation: Missing dates mean the recruiter cannot verify experience level. This is a significant credibility gap.
An unverifiable chronology means recruiters don't trust any of the downstream experience claims either.
Without dates, your resume fails a basic completeness check — most ATS systems will also fail to parse it correctly.
action: Add dates to every role using the format: Month Year – Month Year (or 'Present' for your current role). No exceptions.
Fix your chronology first. Align work history, education, and career breaks into one clear, consistent timeline.
Every role needs: company name, your title, start date, end date (or present), and a location. Without all five, credibility drops.

observation: '{{skill_name}}' is prominently visible in your skills section.
The required skill '{{skill_name}}' appears right where a recruiter scans first — your skills section.
'{{skill_name}}' is surfaced in the top third of your resume, exactly where it needs to be.
interpretation: A recruiter scanning for this core requirement will find it in under one second.
No hunting required. The skill is where the eye goes naturally.
This is keyword placement best practice — visible without reading a single bullet.

observation: '{{skill_name}}' appears in a bullet point in your most recent role but not in your skills section.
The skill '{{skill_name}}' is mentioned in your experience bullets, but is absent from your dedicated skills section.
'{{skill_name}}' shows up in your work history — just not where a recruiter looks first.
interpretation: A recruiter's eye doesn't consistently reach bullet points in a 10-second scan — this skill is at risk of being missed.
Bullet-only placement means the skill depends on the recruiter reading, not scanning. Many won't get that far.
The skill is technically on your resume — but its position reduces its impact significantly.
action: Move '{{skill_name}}' into your skills section so it's visible at a glance.
Add '{{skill_name}}' to your technical skills section. Keep it in the bullet too — double visibility is the goal.
Your skills section is scanned first. '{{skill_name}}' needs to be there, not hidden in experience bullets.

observation: '{{skill_name}}' only appears in an older or less prominent role — it's not surfaced where recruiters look.
The skill '{{skill_name}}' is buried in experience bullets from a previous position.
'{{skill_name}}' is technically on your resume, but deep in your work history where it won't register on a fast scan.
interpretation: A skill in an old role bullet is effectively invisible on a first pass.
Recruiters scanning your skills section and recent experience won't see '{{skill_name}}' — it's categorised as a historical skill, not a current one.
Buried skills fail the recruiter's fast-scan test. If it doesn't appear in the top third, it often doesn't count.
action: Move '{{skill_name}}' into your skills section. If you've used it recently, mention it in your current or most recent role.
Rescue '{{skill_name}}' from the older role and resurface it — either in your skills list or in a more recent bullet.
If '{{skill_name}}' is still relevant and current, treat it as current. Surface it in your skills section immediately.

observation: '{{skill_name}}' is mentioned only once and in passing — it doesn't read as a real proficiency.
The skill '{{skill_name}}' appears as a footnote rather than a feature of your profile.
'{{skill_name}}' is technically present but so lightly referenced it provides no confidence to a recruiter.
interpretation: A single passing mention tells a recruiter you've touched the technology, not that you can be hired for it.
Recruiters aren't just looking for the keyword — they're looking for evidence of use. One passing mention doesn't provide that.
This is the weakest version of having a skill on a resume. It's unlikely to pass a technical screener.
action: If '{{skill_name}}' is genuinely a skill you have, add it to your skills section and reference it with a concrete bullet in your most relevant role.
Back the skill with evidence: add a bullet that shows what you built or did with '{{skill_name}}'.
A skill mentioned in passing is nearly as bad as missing. Surface it properly or cut it.

observation: '{{skill_name}}' does not appear anywhere on your resume.
The required skill '{{skill_name}}' is completely absent from your resume.
There is no mention of '{{skill_name}}' — not in your skills section, not in your bullets, not anywhere.
interpretation: A missing must-have skill is typically a hard rejection filter. Most recruiters stop here.
This is the most critical skill gap — it doesn't just hurt your score, it ends the review for automated screeners.
If '{{skill_name}}' is listed as required in the JD and absent from your resume, ATS scoring will likely disqualify this application.
action: If you have experience with '{{skill_name}}', add it immediately — to your skills section and at least one bullet.
If you genuinely lack '{{skill_name}}', this role may not be the right fit yet. Consider upskilling before applying.
Missing required skills are the single biggest fixable problem on a resume. If you've used '{{skill_name}}' at all, it must be visible.

observation: Your skills are grouped by category with the most relevant skills listed first in each group.
The skills section is well-structured: categorised, ordered by relevance, and free of noise.
Your skills are organised into clear categories with the highest-priority skills leading.
interpretation: A recruiter scanning for core requirements finds them in under one second. This is best practice.
Categorised, ordered skills sections dramatically reduce recruiter effort. This one is optimised.
The structure signals technical maturity — you know which skills matter most for this role.

observation: Your skills are grouped by category but the order doesn't prioritise the most relevant skills.
Your skills are listed in a flat format, but the most relevant ones appear near the top.
The skills section is well-formatted but the internal ordering doesn't optimise for this role.
interpretation: Good structure, minor optimisation left. The recruiter finds the skills — just not instantly.
A flat but ordered list is scannable enough to work. Categories would improve it further.
Ordering matters — recruiters read left-to-right and top-to-bottom. Small reorder, meaningful improvement.
action: Reorder your skills so the most JD-relevant skill appears first in its category — or first on the flat list.
Put your most in-demand skills at the front. A recruiter's eye lands on the first item in each group.
Quick win: move the skills that appear in the JD to the top of your skills section.

observation: Your skills are in a flat list with key skills buried mid-way through.
Technical and soft skills are mixed together in the same section.
Skills are formatted as one per bullet point — functional but space-inefficient.
interpretation: A recruiter scanning fast could easily miss the key skill buried at position 8 in a flat list.
Soft skills mixed into a technical skills section dilute the technical signal. Recruiters scan for Java, not 'Communication'.
Bullet-per-skill lists take up excessive space and can look junior to experienced recruiters.
action: Move your most relevant skills to the top of the list. Better yet, group them: 'Languages: Java, Python, Go'.
Separate technical and soft skills. Create distinct sections or remove soft skills from your skills area entirely.
Switch from bullet-per-skill to a comma-separated list grouped by category. It's more compact and more professional.

observation: Your skills are written in paragraph form rather than a scannable list.
Your skills section includes self-assessed proficiency ratings (e.g. 'Java — Expert').
Your skills list includes version numbers for most technologies — e.g. 'Java 8, Java 11, Java 17'.
Your skills section contains only soft skills — no technical skills are listed.
interpretation: Skills in paragraph form require reading rather than scanning. In a 10-second review, prose skills are ignored.
Self-assessed proficiency ratings carry zero weight with technical recruiters — and create awkward questions in interviews.
Version numbers belong in interviews, not skills sections. They add visual noise without adding useful signal.
A technical role with no technical skills in the skills section is a critical miss — the section is doing nothing for you.
action: Reformat your skills as a comma-separated list grouped by category. Remove all sentences.
Remove all proficiency ratings. Let your experience bullets demonstrate your depth instead.
Strip the version numbers. List 'Java', not 'Java 8 / Java 11 / Java 17'. Recruiters know Java evolved.
Replace soft skills with your actual technical stack. Soft skills can live in your summary or be cut entirely.

observation: No dedicated skills section was found on your resume.
There is no skills section — technical keywords are only present in experience bullets.
Your resume is missing a skills section entirely.
interpretation: Without a skills section, a recruiter has to hunt through bullet points for technical keywords. Most won't.
ATS systems rely heavily on skills sections for keyword extraction. Without one, your resume will score poorly in automated screening.
The absence of a skills section is the single biggest structural problem this resume has for technical role applications.
action: Add a dedicated 'Technical Skills' or 'Skills' section near the top of your resume — above or immediately after your summary.
This is a non-negotiable fix. Create a skills section today: group your technologies by category and list them clearly.
Place a skills section in the top third of your resume. This is the highest-impact structural change you can make.

observation: Your experience bullets are specific, quantified, and lead with strong action verbs.
Most bullets follow the impact formula: action verb + specific task + measurable outcome.
Your bullets demonstrate what you built, how you built it, and the result — the gold standard.
interpretation: This is what strong engineering bullets look like. A recruiter reads these and sees evidence, not claims.
Quantified bullets give a technical recruiter something to anchor a comp discussion on — they signal seniority.
Specific, outcome-focused bullets survive both the ATS scan and the human review. These work.

observation: Your bullets are specific and action-oriented but lack quantified outcomes.
Good action verbs and technical specificity — but most bullets don't state the result or scale.
Your experience bullets describe what you did clearly, but not the impact of doing it.
interpretation: Specific bullets are good — but 'reduced latency' is weaker than 'reduced latency by 40%'. Numbers close the loop.
A recruiter can see what you did. They can't see the scale or significance without numbers.
One step away from excellent. Adding even rough metrics transforms good bullets into standout ones.
action: Add a result or scale to your strongest bullets. Even rough numbers help: 'reduced load time by ~30%' is better than none.
For every bullet, ask: so what? The answer is what's missing. Add it.
Review your top 3 bullets and add one metric to each. Percentage improvement, scale (users, requests, records), or time saved.

observation: Most of your bullets describe responsibilities rather than achievements.
Your experience bullets use weak verbs like 'assisted', 'supported', 'helped with', or 'involved in'.
The language in your bullets is vague — it's hard to gauge the scale or complexity of your work.
interpretation: Responsibility bullets ('Responsible for...') describe a job description, not a candidate. Recruiters see this as undifferentiated.
Weak verbs understate your contribution. 'Assisted' implies you were peripheral. 'Engineered' or 'Built' implies you were central.
Vague bullets leave the recruiter guessing at your seniority level — and they'll guess low.
action: Rewrite 'responsible for X' as 'built / designed / led / engineered X that achieved Y'.
Cut every 'assisted', 'supported', and 'helped'. Replace with the direct verb: what did YOU do?
Add specificity to your vaguest bullets: what was the scale, the tech used, the team size, the outcome?

observation: Your bullets read as a duty list rather than an achievement record.
No technical context is mentioned in your experience bullets — tools and technologies are absent.
Most bullets are a single short line with no context, scale, or outcome.
interpretation: A list of duties tells a recruiter what your job was, not whether you were good at it.
Experience bullets with no technical context are difficult to evaluate for a technical role — the recruiter has nothing to assess.
Short, decontextualised bullets signal junior thinking about how to present work — regardless of the work's actual quality.
action: For each bullet, add: what you built, what tech you used, and what happened as a result. Three elements, one sentence.
Add the tech stack to every bullet where it's relevant. Recruiters need to see the tools you actually used.
Expand your shortest bullets. Context is not padding — it's evidence.

observation: Your work experience is written in paragraph form rather than bullet points.
Some roles have only a single bullet — not enough to represent your contribution.
One or more roles have no descriptions at all — just the job title and dates.
interpretation: Paragraph-format experience is very difficult to scan. In 10 seconds, a recruiter won't read a paragraph — they'll skip it.
A single bullet per role tells a recruiter almost nothing. It suggests either junior experience or a failure to articulate value.
Roles with no description are dead weight — they add to your timeline but subtract from your credibility.
action: Reformat all experience into bullet points. 3–5 bullets per role is the target.
Add at least 3 bullets to each significant role. Each bullet = one achievement or responsibility.
Every role deserves a description. Even a brief one. 'Led backend development for X feature' is infinitely better than nothing.

observation: {{company_name}} is a well-recognised top-tier company.
{{company_name}} is an immediately recognisable name — no context needed.
Your current employer, {{company_name}}, is a strong signal on its own.
interpretation: Engineering bar, scale, and culture are all inferred without reading a word. Strong trust signal.
Top-tier company names do credibility work passively — recruiters read the rest of your resume with a different lens.
A recognisable employer is the fastest possible way to establish credibility before any detail is read.

observation: {{company_name}} is known in the industry, even if not universally recognised.
{{company_name}} is a credible name — most experienced recruiters will know it.
Your employer is a recognised scale-up — provides some credibility context.
interpretation: Clear enough context that a positive signal is conveyed. Mild trust indicator.
Not FAANG, but not unknown either. Most technical recruiters will have a frame of reference.
Decent company recognition. Unlikely to be a barrier — but won't be a major positive either.

observation: {{company_name}} isn't widely known, but you've provided some context in your description.
Your employer isn't immediately recognisable, though the role description adds some framing.
{{company_name}} is unfamiliar to most recruiters but the surrounding context helps.
interpretation: Unknown name, but the descriptor partially fills the gap. It reduces the ambiguity without eliminating it.
A recruiter outside your industry may still lack a frame of reference even with context.
Providing some context is better than none — but a more explicit descriptor would do more work.
action: Add a brief descriptor directly after the company name: e.g. '{{company_name}} (Series B fintech, 200 engineers)'.
A bracketed descriptor — stage, industry, scale — gives any recruiter an instant frame of reference.
One line of context makes an unknown company legible. Add it to every employer a recruiter might not recognise.

observation: {{company_name}} is an early-stage company with no additional context provided.
Your employer is listed with no descriptor — a recruiter has no frame of reference.
{{company_name}} isn't recognisable and no context is given to help a recruiter understand the engineering environment.
interpretation: No company tier signal. A recruiter has no way to infer team size, scale, or engineering standards.
Without context, an unknown employer is a neutral-to-negative signal — uncertainty defaults to scepticism.
Recruiters who can't place your employer will spend less time on your resume, not more.
action: Add a descriptor in brackets after the company name: e.g. '{{company_name}} (early-stage SaaS startup, 20-person engineering team)'.
Give recruiters the context they need: stage, industry, and scale. Three pieces of information — one parenthetical.
Don't let an unknown company name be an invisible barrier. Name the category: fintech, healthtech, B2B SaaS — whatever applies.

observation: One or more roles list no company name — just a title and dates.
Every employer on your resume is unrecognised and none have contextual descriptors.
Company information is missing or severely incomplete across your work history.
interpretation: Missing company names are a red flag — they suggest something is being obscured, even if unintentionally.
A resume where every employer is unknown and uncontextualised gives a recruiter nothing to calibrate against.
Without any company credibility signal, a recruiter has no external validation of your experience claims.
action: Add a company name to every role — no exceptions. A recruiter needs to know where you worked.
Add a descriptor to every employer a recruiter wouldn't recognise: industry, stage, and rough size.
Company context is one of the fastest trust signals on a resume. Invest the two lines needed to add it everywhere.

observation: Your employment history has no unexplained gaps.
All periods between roles are either continuous or explicitly labelled.
Your work history is complete and continuous — no unexplained breaks.
interpretation: A clean timeline removes a common recruiter concern before it's even raised.
No gaps means no distracting questions. The recruiter's attention stays on your skills and achievements.
Continuous or fully explained history is the baseline expectation — you meet it cleanly.

observation: There's a brief gap in your work history that is labelled and explained.
A short career break appears in your timeline — you've noted its reason.
A {{gap_duration}} gap between roles is present and accounted for.
interpretation: A labelled gap raises no flags. It's the unexplained ones that concern recruiters.
Brief gaps are normal. The label is what makes the difference between a red flag and a non-event.
Explained breaks are visible but ignorable. You've handled this correctly.

observation: There is a {{gap_duration}} gap in your employment history with no explanation.
A break of approximately {{gap_duration}} between {{gap_start}} and your next role is unexplained.
A short-to-medium employment gap appears without any label or context.
interpretation: Recruiters notice unexplained gaps, even short ones — their imagination fills the void.
A brief unexplained gap is unlikely to be a dealbreaker, but it's an unnecessary distraction.
The gap itself isn't the issue — the absence of an explanation is.
action: Add a one-line entry for this period: 'Career break — travel / personal reasons / upskilling / parental leave'. A label makes it a non-issue.
Label the gap. Whatever the reason — health, family, relocation, study — name it. Recruiters respect honesty over omission.
A labelled gap is a closed question. An unlabelled one is an open one. Close it.

observation: There is a {{gap_duration}} gap in your work history starting around {{gap_start}} with no explanation.
A significant employment gap of {{gap_duration}} is unexplained on your resume.
Your employment history has a notable break of {{gap_duration}} that a recruiter will ask about.
interpretation: A gap of this length without explanation is one of the most common recruiter pause points.
Unexplained gaps of 6+ months invite speculation — and speculation is almost always worse than the truth.
This will come up in every interview. Better to own it on the resume than be asked to explain it cold.
action: Add a labelled entry for this period. Options: 'Career break — parental leave', 'Career break — health', 'Sabbatical — personal development / travel', 'Freelance work / consulting'.
A single honest line removes the question entirely. Recruiters respect self-awareness far more than unexplained omissions.
If you upskilled during this period, list it explicitly as an entry with dates. Courses, projects, certifications — all count.

observation: Multiple significant unexplained gaps appear across your employment history.
Your work history has over a year of unaccounted-for time spread across several breaks.
The pattern of unexplained employment gaps in your history creates a significant credibility concern.
interpretation: Multiple unexplained gaps compound — each one adds to a pattern that recruiters find difficult to overlook.
The cumulative unaccounted time on this resume is significant enough to be a primary rejection factor.
At this level, the gaps aren't background noise — they're the loudest signal on the page.
action: Address every gap. Every single one needs a label, even a brief one. This is not optional.
Work backwards through your history and add a one-line entry for every period over 1 month that isn't currently explained.
If you have freelance, consulting, or project work during any of these periods, document it as a role. Even informal work counts if it's real.

observation: Your work history follows a clean reverse-chronological order with consistent date formatting.
The timeline on your resume is clear, logical, and easy to verify.
Dates are formatted consistently throughout and the chronology is easy to follow.
interpretation: Clean chronology removes friction from the experience verification process.
A recruiter can calculate your YOE, identify your progression, and spot any concerns in seconds.
Consistent dating and logical ordering is baseline professionalism — you've got it right.

observation: Your date formatting is inconsistent — some roles show month and year, others just year.
Some roles are missing month-level detail, making the timeline harder to verify.
Your resume uses a functional format that groups skills rather than showing a chronological history.
interpretation: Inconsistent dates mean a recruiter has to mentally standardise your timeline — added friction.
Year-only dates obscure short-tenure roles and make YOE calculations unreliable.
Functional formats hide your career progression. Most recruiters distrust them because of that ambiguity.
action: Standardise all dates to: Month Year – Month Year (e.g. Jan 2020 – Mar 2023).
Use month-year for every role. Year-only formatting is associated with gaps or short tenures being hidden.
Switch to reverse-chronological format. Most recruiters and ATS systems expect it, and functional formats raise red flags.

observation: Your work history contains overlapping dates that make the timeline impossible to follow.
The chronology on your resume contains apparent contradictions — roles that don't add up sequentially.
Dates across your work history are inconsistent in ways that undermine the entire timeline.
interpretation: Overlapping dates suggest errors, exaggeration, or a poorly formatted resume — none of which are good.
A recruiter who spots a chronological contradiction will question the entire resume, not just that role.
Chronology errors are credibility killers. If the dates can't be trusted, nothing downstream can be trusted.
action: Fix every date error before submitting this resume anywhere. Start with the overlaps and work outwards.
Print your timeline and verify it manually. Every role should end before the next begins.
Align work history, education, and career breaks into one coherent timeline. Every period must be accounted for.

observation: Your {{degree}} from {{institution}} is directly relevant to this role.
A relevant degree from a well-regarded institution — {{institution}} — strengthens your application.
Your educational background aligns well with the technical requirements of this role.
interpretation: A relevant degree from a credible institution is a passive trust signal — it works without anyone noticing it.
Education rarely wins a technical role, but a strong relevant degree removes a background concern.
Your academic background does quiet supporting work throughout the review.

observation: Your {{degree}} is in a related field — solid foundation for this type of role.
Your degree is relevant even if not directly matched — the underlying discipline applies.
A {{degree}} in a cognate field provides good academic grounding for this position.
interpretation: Not an exact match, but close enough that education won't be a concern for experienced reviewers.
Related field degrees are viewed positively in most technical hiring contexts.
Your education contributes a supporting signal — not a defining one, but not a gap either.

observation: Your {{degree}} is in an unrelated field with no technical bridge courses or certifications listed.
Your educational background doesn't directly connect to this role's requirements.
The degree on your resume is from a different discipline — the connection to this role isn't explicit.
interpretation: An unrelated degree isn't disqualifying, but it adds a question mark that experience and skills need to answer.
For technical roles, an unrelated degree is a flag a recruiter notes — and expects your experience to override.
The gap between your degree and this role is visible. Compensating signals (certs, projects, experience) must close it.
action: Add any relevant certifications or self-study to your education section to bridge the gap.
If you've taken any relevant courses, bootcamps, or completed significant projects, list them under education or a separate 'Training' section.
Your experience must carry this application. Make sure your skills and bullet points fully compensate for the education mismatch.

observation: No education section is present on your resume.
Your education section is incomplete — institution or degree details are missing.
Education details are absent or too sparse to be informative.
interpretation: A missing education section raises questions — especially for roles that have a degree preference.
Incomplete education listings read as either an oversight or deliberate omission. Both are flags.
Even if your experience is strong, most ATS systems check for an education field. Its absence can cause scoring issues.
action: Add an education section, even if brief. List your highest qualification, the institution, and the year of completion.
If you don't have a traditional degree, list any relevant certifications, bootcamps, or professional qualifications instead.
Incomplete information is worse than complete honest information. Fill in what you have.

observation: Your {{cert_name}} certification is directly relevant to this role and currently valid.
A current {{cert_name}} is listed — a strong supporting signal for your technical credentials.
Your certifications include {{cert_name}}, which is highly valued in this hiring context.
interpretation: Industry-recognised certifications provide third-party validation of your skills — they're worth more than self-claims.
A current, relevant cert tells a recruiter you've invested in formal validation of your knowledge.
Certifications in the right areas are ATS keywords and trust signals simultaneously.

observation: You have certifications listed, though they're not directly aligned with this role's requirements.
{{cert_name}} is present but is from an adjacent area rather than a direct match.
Your certifications signal commitment to learning, even if not perfectly targeted.
interpretation: Adjacent certifications contribute a small positive signal — they show commitment to professional development.
Not a direct keyword hit, but demonstrates a structured approach to learning.
Partially relevant certs are better than none — they fill gaps in the profile rather than leaving voids.
action: If you're pursuing any JD-relevant certifications, add them in progress: e.g. 'AWS Solutions Architect (in progress, expected Q3 2025)'.
Prioritise getting certified in the specific areas this role demands — it directly addresses a visible gap.
Add any in-progress certifications with expected completion dates. Showing momentum matters.

observation: Your certifications are present but significantly out of date.
No certifications are listed despite this role typically expecting them.
Certifications on your resume have lapsed — their value is significantly reduced.
interpretation: Outdated certifications can signal stagnation rather than strength — a recruiter notes when you last invested in formal learning.
For a role that values or expects certification, their absence is a visible gap against similarly qualified candidates.
Expired certifications raise the question of whether you've kept your skills current.
action: Renew or update certifications that have lapsed — especially if they're in core areas for this role.
If you can't renew immediately, pursue the most relevant certification for this role and list it as 'in progress'.
Add the year to every certification. Current certs include a year; expired ones look current without one — don't mislead.

observation: This role specifically requires or strongly prefers {{cert_name}} — it's absent from your resume.
A required certification is not present on your resume.
The JD lists {{cert_name}} as a key requirement. It doesn't appear anywhere on your resume.
interpretation: A missing required certification is a hard filter in many hiring processes — ATS systems will score this against you.
When a cert is listed as required (not preferred), its absence can be an automatic screen-out in structured hiring pipelines.
This gap is addressable but needs to be addressed quickly — or acknowledged proactively in your application.
action: If you can obtain {{cert_name}} quickly, prioritise it before applying to more roles of this type.
If you're applying anyway, note in your cover letter that you're pursuing {{cert_name}} and give an expected date.
Add {{cert_name}} as 'in progress' if you've started the process. Showing initiative partially offsets the gap.

observation: Your resume is {{page_count}} page(s) — appropriate for your level of experience.
At {{page_count}} pages, the length is well-calibrated to your career stage.
Your resume fits into {{page_count}} page(s) without padding or compression.
interpretation: Length signals judgement. Right-sized resumes tell a recruiter you understand what matters.
A {{page_count}}-page resume at your experience level is exactly what's expected.
No length flags — the recruiter can focus on the content, not the format.

observation: At {{page_count}} pages, your resume is slightly longer than ideal for your experience level.
Your resume is on the shorter side — some relevant content may have been cut.
The resume runs {{page_count}} pages, which is borderline for a role at this level.
interpretation: Slightly long resumes make recruiters work harder to find the signal in the noise.
A short resume can leave a recruiter without enough to evaluate — they fill gaps with uncertainty.
Length isn't a dealbreaker, but it's a friction signal — either too much or too little.
action: Target a tighter {{page_count}} pages. Cut oldest roles to 1–2 bullets. Remove generic skills.
If you're under 2 pages and have 5+ years of experience, consider expanding the bullet detail on your most recent role.
Review every bullet and remove any that don't directly add to your case for this specific role.

observation: Your resume is {{page_count}} pages — significantly longer than the industry standard.
At {{page_count}} pages, the resume will not be read in full by most recruiters.
Your resume is a single brief page with limited content — too short for the experience level this role requires.
interpretation: A {{page_count}}-page resume signals poor editorial judgment. Recruiters see it as an inability to prioritise.
Most recruiters spend 30–60 seconds per resume. A {{page_count}}-page document means pages 3+ will rarely be seen.
A very short resume for a senior role suggests either hidden experience or weak self-presentation.
action: Cut to 2 pages maximum. Older roles (5+ years ago) should have 0–2 bullets. Roles from 10+ years ago may not need to appear at all.
Every line should earn its place. Remove: old roles, generic skills, soft skill sections, and any content not relevant to this JD.
Expand your most recent roles with 4–5 strong, specific bullets. Quality depth beats surface breadth.

observation: Your resume uses a clean, single-column layout with standard fonts and generous whitespace.
The layout is ATS-safe, visually clean, and easy to scan.
Your formatting is professional, consistent, and optimised for both human and automated review.
interpretation: ATS-safe formatting means your resume is parsed correctly — no keyword extraction failures.
A clean layout lets the content do the work. No formatting quirks pull the recruiter's attention away.
This is the format that works universally — in every ATS system, on every screen, in every inbox.

observation: Your resume uses a multi-column layout that may not parse correctly in ATS systems.
Heavy formatting — icons, graphics, or colour backgrounds — is present and may cause ATS parsing failures.
The font size in places drops below 10pt, making the document difficult to scan quickly.
Spacing and formatting are inconsistent across sections — the layout needs standardising.
interpretation: Multi-column layouts break most ATS parsers — skills and experience from the second column are often lost entirely.
Design-heavy resumes may look great as PDFs but fail completely when parsed by automated systems.
Small fonts and inconsistent spacing slow down the recruiter's eye — even a strong resume reads as more effort.
action: Switch to a single-column layout. Multi-column formats fail ATS parsing and are not worth the aesthetic trade-off.
Remove all icons, photos, graphics, and coloured backgrounds. Plain text and clean layout wins in every context.
Increase body font to a minimum of 10.5pt (ideally 11pt). Your resume shouldn't require squinting.
Pick one date format, one bullet style, and one font — then apply them consistently throughout.

observation: Your resume appears to use tables or text boxes for layout — these frequently fail ATS parsing.
Key content may be in the header or footer of the document — ATS systems often skip these areas.
Your resume may be image-based or contain embedded graphics that make text extraction impossible.
The layout structure of this document will likely cause significant ATS parsing failures.
interpretation: ATS systems extract text linearly. Tables and text boxes break this extraction — your content may be partially or completely lost.
Content in headers and footers is routinely ignored by ATS parsers — contact info stored there may never be read.
An image-based resume is invisible to ATS systems. Every keyword, every skill, every company name — unreadable by automation.
Regardless of how strong your experience is, an unparseable resume will be automatically scored at or near zero.
action: Rebuild your resume in a simple word processor or a trusted resume tool. Remove all tables, text boxes, and graphics.
Move all contact information out of the header/footer and into the main document body.
Use plain text formatting only: section headings, bullet points, and bold for emphasis. Nothing more.
Test your resume by copying and pasting it into a plain text editor. If the structure breaks completely, your ATS submission will fail.

observation: Your contact section is complete: email, phone, LinkedIn, and location are all present.
All key contact details are present and professional.
Your contact information covers every channel a recruiter might use to reach you.
interpretation: A complete contact section means zero friction from the moment a recruiter decides to follow up.
Nothing is missing. A recruiter doesn't have to hunt for how to reach you.
Professionally formatted contact information signals attention to detail — even before the content is read.

observation: Email and phone are present. LinkedIn or location is missing.
Your contact info covers the basics but is missing a useful secondary channel.
Core reachability is covered, though a LinkedIn profile or location note would add context.
interpretation: Email and phone are sufficient for basic reachability — the gap is minor.
LinkedIn is increasingly expected at senior levels — its absence is a small but noticeable gap.
Location context helps recruiters with relocation or remote screening — worth adding even as just 'Dublin, IE' or 'Remote'.
action: Add your LinkedIn URL. Customise it: linkedin.com/in/yourname — not the default character string.
Add a location indicator even if brief: 'Dublin, IE' or 'Remote — open to relocation' provides useful context.
Both LinkedIn and location take 30 seconds to add. They're standard expectations for senior applications.

observation: Your email address uses an unprofessional handle that may reflect poorly.
A phone number is missing from your contact details.
Contact information is incomplete — a recruiter may struggle to reach you.
interpretation: An unprofessional email (e.g. partylad99@...) is a trivially easy thing to fix — and a surprisingly easy thing to be judged for.
Missing a phone number reduces recruiter options. Some prefer calling to emailing for initial outreach.
Incomplete contact info creates friction at the worst possible moment — when a recruiter actually wants to act.
action: Create a professional email address if you don't have one: firstname.lastname@gmail.com is the standard.
Add your phone number — including the country code for international applications.
Audit your contact section: you need a professional email, phone, LinkedIn, and location indicator at minimum.

observation: No contact information was found on your resume.
Critical contact details are missing — a recruiter cannot follow up on this application.
The contact section is either absent or so incomplete it provides no usable information.
interpretation: A resume without contact information cannot lead to an interview. This is a functional failure of the document.
Even if every other element of this resume is excellent, missing contact info makes it impossible to act on.
This is the most critical administrative fix on this resume.
action: Add your contact information immediately: full name, professional email, phone number, LinkedIn, and city/country.
Place contact info at the very top of the resume — it must be the first thing a recruiter sees.
Do not submit this resume until contact details are present and correct.

observation: No credibility anomalies or inconsistencies were detected.
Your resume is internally consistent — no red flags or impossible claims.
Everything checks out: dates, skill ages, company claims, and tenure lengths are all plausible.
interpretation: A clean anomaly check means your resume can be read at face value. No trust erosion.
Consistency across all signals means a recruiter can build a coherent picture of your background.
No flags here — this is the baseline every resume should meet.

observation: {{anomaly_detail}} — this claim is inconsistent with known information.
A skill is claimed with more years of experience than the technology has existed.
A pattern of very short tenures (under 3 months) appears across multiple roles.
interpretation: Anomalies like this undermine credibility across the entire resume — not just the specific claim.
A recruiter who catches a factual impossibility will question everything else on the page.
Short tenure patterns without explanation signal either poor judgment or an unstable career. Both prompt concerns.
action: Correct {{anomaly_detail}} immediately — a single credibility error can invalidate an otherwise strong resume.
Fix the years of experience claimed for this skill — the technology simply hasn't existed that long.
If you've had short tenures, add a one-line context note for each: acquisition, project-based contract, or restructuring.

observation: Your resume contains directly contradictory information — {{anomaly_detail}}.
Two roles on your resume overlap in a way that is logistically impossible.
A claimed qualification or credential cannot be verified as stated.
interpretation: Direct contradictions are the fastest credibility killers on a resume. A recruiter who spots one will stop reading.
Impossible overlaps suggest fabrication — even if the error is unintentional, the impression is serious.
Credibility anomalies at this level are often automatic rejection triggers — no further review takes place.
action: Fix {{anomaly_detail}} before submitting this resume anywhere. This is the highest priority change on this document.
Verify every date, every claimed tenure, and every qualification before this resume goes to another recruiter.
Have someone you trust review the timeline and all factual claims independently — you need a second pair of eyes on this.

observation: Your resume closely mirrors the language and priorities of this job description.
Core JD keywords appear prominently in your summary, skills section, and experience bullets.
The framing and language of your resume is well-aligned with how this role is described.
interpretation: High keyword alignment means strong ATS scoring and immediate visual relevance for human reviewers.
A resume that speaks the JD's language tells a recruiter: this candidate understands what we need.
Tailored resumes outperform generic ones at every stage — from ATS to final interview.

observation: Your resume has reasonable keyword alignment with this JD, but some core terms are missing or underrepresented.
Several JD priorities are reflected in your resume, though not all.
Moderate alignment with the job description — stronger than average but not fully optimised.
interpretation: Good enough to pass an ATS threshold in most cases, but specific keyword gaps could cost points.
A recruiter will see relevance — but may also notice the missing terms against the JD.
Close to tailored. A targeted review of the JD against your resume would quickly identify the gaps.
action: Read the JD and highlight every term that appears more than once — these are the priority keywords. Check each against your resume.
Add missing JD terms where you have legitimate experience. Natural integration, not keyword stuffing.
Pay attention to exact phrasing — 'product roadmap' and 'roadmap planning' are treated differently by some ATS systems.

observation: Your resume appears to be a general version rather than one tailored to this specific role.
There is low alignment between the language used in your resume and the terminology in this JD.
Core keywords from the job description are largely absent from your resume.
interpretation: A generic resume sent to a targeted role is one of the most common reasons for early rejection.
ATS systems score keyword overlap — low alignment means a low automated score regardless of your experience.
A recruiter reading a generic resume for a specific role doesn't see fit — they see effort not invested.
action: Spend 20 minutes tailoring this resume to this specific JD before applying. It is the highest-ROI activity in any job search.
Copy the JD's exact terminology where you legitimately have the experience. Don't paraphrase what the JD says clearly.
At minimum, update your summary and skills section to reflect this role's specific language. That alone moves the needle significantly.

observation: This resume shows very little alignment with the target job description.
The framing and terminology of your resume is from a different domain than the one this role requires.
Critical JD keywords are almost entirely absent — this resume is not optimised for this role.
interpretation: At this level of misalignment, the ATS score will likely be below the threshold for human review.
A recruiter seeing this resume for this role will not recognise the fit — the work is left entirely to them.
Submitting a resume with this level of misalignment is unlikely to produce results regardless of the underlying experience.
action: Do not submit this resume to this role without significant tailoring first.
Start with the JD. Underline every required skill and responsibility. Then check every underlined item against your resume and address each gap.
Rewrite your summary entirely for this role. Use the JD's language. Then update your skills section and your top 3 bullets.

composite_score.description: Weighted average of all signal scores. Used to generate the top-level feedback statement.

headline: This is a strong resume — well-structured, well-targeted, and ready to compete seriously for this role.
Your resume is in excellent shape for this application. The core signals are working in your favour.
Strong overall. This resume will pass most filters and give a recruiter reason to keep reading.
closing: Focus on the few remaining items above and this resume is highly competitive.
Small refinements only — the fundamentals are solid.
This resume is working hard for you. The gaps above are polish, not reconstruction.

headline: Your resume is solid and will get through many filters, but there are clear improvements that would make it significantly stronger.
A good foundation — but the gaps identified above are meaningful enough to address before wide distribution.
Above average, but not where it needs to be for a highly competitive role. The issues above are fixable in a few hours.
closing: Address the priority items above and this resume moves from good to strong.
The improvements above are targeted and achievable — prioritise the high-weight signals first.
Don't let fixable issues cost you opportunities. The changes above are worth making.

headline: Your resume has real gaps that are likely costing you interviews. The issues below are addressable but need attention.
This resume is functional but underperforming. A focused revision would significantly change outcomes.
There are several meaningful issues with this resume that a recruiter is likely to notice. They're worth fixing before applying further.
closing: Start with the highest-weight signals above — title, skills visibility, and summary. Fix those first.
A few hours of focused revision could meaningfully change your application outcomes.
Don't distribute this resume further until the priority issues above are resolved.

headline: This resume has significant structural and content issues that will result in rejection at most filter stages.
There are fundamental problems with this resume that go beyond light editing — it needs a substantive revision.
As currently structured, this resume is unlikely to pass ATS screening or a 10-second recruiter scan for this role.
closing: Treat this as a full revision, not a light edit. Start with the critical items and work down.
The issues above are serious enough that applying without addressing them will produce poor results.
Block out 2–3 hours and rebuild from the high-priority signals above. The effort will be worth it.

headline: This resume needs a complete overhaul before it should be submitted to any role.
As it stands, this resume will not pass basic screening for this role or most others like it.
The issues with this resume are fundamental — structure, content, and targeting all need significant work.
closing: Do not submit this resume further until the critical issues above are resolved.
Start fresh with a clean template and use the feedback above as your rebuild checklist.
This is a significant undertaking, but it's worth doing properly. A strong resume is the highest-leverage career investment you can make.
